title: The Fleeing Atalanta
snippet: Maier's introductory apparatus presents Atalanta Fugiens as an exercise in multi-sensory pedagogy: the subtitle promises emblems for the eyes, fugues for the ears, and epigrams for the intellect, engaging sight, he a ring, and reason simultaneously in the pursuit of Nature's secr

title: Emblem I — His nurse is the Earth
snippet: The wind carried it in its belly. · Nutrix ejus terra est · Maier develops the Tabula Smaragdina's declaration that 'the wind carried it in its belly' into an extended analogy between alchemical generation and natural gestation. He argues that the philosophical substance must first be conceived in air (the volatile spirit) before being no

title: Emblem II — Its nurse is the Earth
snippet: Its nurse is the Earth. · Maier continues the the me of Emblem I, now emphasizing that the earth is specifically the nurse, not the mother, of the philosophical substance. He distinguishes between generation (which occurs through the volatile principle, the wind) and nourishment (which requires the fixed,

title: Emblem III — Go to the woman who washes the sheets
snippet: Go to the woman who washes the sheets and do as she does. · Maier argues that the alchemical purification process mirrors the everyday work of washing dirty linen: just as earth-stains on fabric are removed by water and then dried by sun and wind, so the impurities of the philosophical substance must be cleansed through repeated washing a

title: Emblem IV — Join brother and sister
snippet: Join brother and sister and give them the cup of love. · Maier frames the alchemical coniunctio as a marriage between brother and sister — the masculine and feminine principles of the same substance — who must be united by the 'cup of love,' the solvent that dissolves both into a single new body. He draws on Oedipus's unwitting marriag

title: Emblem V — Woman suckling the toad
snippet: Put a toad to the breasts of a woman, that she may suckle it, and the woman may die and the toad grow big from the milk. · Maier frames the alchemical operation as gendered labor, arguing that the masculine principle be gets and governs while the feminine conceives, bears, and nourishes. He develops the toad as a figure for the volatile,

title: Emblem VI — Sow your gold in the white foliated earth
snippet: Sow your gold in the white foliated earth. · Maier develops an extended agricultural analogy: just as the farmer must sow grain in prepared soil to reap a harvest, the alchemist must 'sow' philosophical gold in 'white foliated earth' — a specially prepared mercurial substrate — to produce the Stone. Drawing on Plato's divis

title: Emblem VII — Young bird flying from nest and falling back
snippet: A young bird flies up from the nest and falls back into the nest again. · Maier describes a species of bird that flies upward from its nest only to fall back again repeatedly, developing this as an analogy for the alchemical process of sublimation and condensation. He argues that the volatile philosophical substance must be repeatedly dri

title: Emblem VIII — Emblem VIII
snippet: Take the egg and pierce it with a fiery sword. · Maier develops the egg as a figure for the sealed vessel containing the prima materia, arguing that it must be pierced — that is, opened to the action of fire — with a fiery sword to release and transform its contents. The discourse draws on the widespread alchemical tradition of

title: Emblem IX — Emblem IX
snippet: Fix the old man to the tree in the garden of the dew, and the old man becomes young again. · Maier argues that the old man represents the fixed, aged substance of the philosophical body that must be rejuvenated through exposure to celestial dew — the volatile, purifying spirit descending from above. He develops a parallel between this ima

title: Emblem X — Emblem X
snippet: Give fire to fire, Mercury to Mercury, and that is enough for you. · Maier develops the principle that like produces like: fire must be added to fire, and mercury to mercury, for the philosophical work to succeed. He argues against the common error of mixing incompatible substances, insisting that the alchemist must recognize the common n

title: Emblem XI — Emblem XI
snippet: Make Latona white and tear up the books. · Maier commands two simultaneous actions: whiten Latona and tear up the books. He develops Latona as a figure for the imperfect philosophical body — an alloy of Sol and Luna stained with black impurities — that must be purified through the albedo. De Jong traces the name Latona to

title: Emblem XII — Emblem XII
snippet: Make Latona white and tear up the books. · Maier continues the argument of Emblem XI, now emphasizing the bewildering variety and obscurity of alchemical writings as the reason books must be abandoned. He argues that the sheer volume of contradictory texts drives seekers to despair, and that the only path forward is direc

title: Emblem XIII — Dropsical ore washed in Jordan
snippet: The ore of the philosophers is dropsical and wants to be washed seven times in the river, just as Naaman the leper washed in the Jordan. · Maier draws on the biblical story of Naaman the leper, healed by washing seven times in the Jordan, as an allegory for the purification of the philosophical ore. He argues that the 'dropsical' stone —

title: Emblem XIV — Emblem XIV
snippet: This is the dragon that devours its own tail. · Maier develops the ancient image of the serpent devouring another serpent to become a dragon, an allegory for the dissolution of one metallic substance by another of similar but more active nature. He argues that in antiquity the serpent represented both poison and medicine — the

title: Emblem XV — Emblem XV
snippet: Let the work of the potter, consisting of dry and wet, teach you. · Maier presents the potter's craft as a model for the alchemical work, arguing that the artisan who shapes clay from wet and dry elements teaches the principles governing the philosophical operation. He develops the analogy between the potter's sequence — moistening, formi

title: Emblem XVI — Winged and wingless lion
snippet: The feathers one lion has not, the other has. · Maier develops the two lions as figures for the volatile and fixed principles of the alchemical work. The winged lion represents the philosophical mercury — fugitive, aerial, unstable — while the wingless lion is the philosophical sulphur — earthy, fixed, enduring. He argues that

title: Emblem XVII — Fourfold fire-ball
snippet: A fourfold fire-ball controls this work. · Maier argues that the alchemical work requires not one fire but four distinct types, each governing a different phase of the operation. He identifies these as the fire of the lamp (external heat), the fire of the ash-bath (gentle, sustained warmth), the fire of the substance it s

title: Emblem XVIII — Emblem XVIII
snippet: Fire likes making things fiery, but not, like gold, making gold. · Maier continues the fire the me from Emblem XVII, arguing that fire delights in making things partake of its own fiery nature but can not directly transmute base metals into gold. He develops a distinction between fire as agent of transformation and gold as the goal: fire

title: Emblem XIX — Emblem XIX
snippet: If you kill one of the four, every body will be dead immediately. · Maier develops the idea that the four elements or components of the philosophical substance are so intimately connected that destroying one annihilates all. He argues that this demonstrates their essential unity — they are not truly four separate things but four aspects o

title: Emblem XX — Emblem XX
snippet: Nature teaches Nature, Nature conquers Nature, Nature rules Nature. · Maier articulates one of alchemy's foundational maxims: Nature teaches Nature, Nature conquers Nature, Nature rules Nature. He argues that the alchemist must learn by observing natural processes, then apply those lessons to guide and finally master the transformations o

title: Emblem XXI — Circle-square-triangle-circle formula
snippet: Make a circle out of a man and a woman, out of this a square, out of this a triangle, make a circle, and you will have the Philosopher's Stone. · Maier develops the famous geometric formula attributed to the Rosarium Philosophorum: make a circle from a man and woman (the coniunctio of opposites), transform this into a square (the four ele

title: Emblem XXII — Emblem XXII
snippet: When you have obtained the white lead, then do woman's work, that is to say: cook. · Maier instructs that once the white lead (the product of the albedo) has been obtained, the alchemist must perform 'women's work' — that is, cook the substance with steady, patient heat. He develops cooking as the central metaphor for the post-albedo oper

title: Emblem XXIII — Emblem XXIII
snippet: It rains gold when Pallas is born at Rhodes. · Maier develops the mythological tradition that golden rain fell on Rhodes when Pallas Athena was born from the head of Zeus, interpreting this as an allegory for the moment when the philosophical gold descends or precipitates during the citrinitas stage. He argues that just as At

title: Emblem XXIV — Alchemical King (garbed)
snippet: The wolf devoured the king, and after the wolf had been burnt, it returned the king to life. · Maier narrates a two-stage allegory: the wolf devours the king (antimony dissolves gold), then the wolf is burned, returning the king to life purified and renewed. He develops this as the central nigredo-to-rubedo sequence: the base, voracious s

title: Emblem XXV — Emblem XXV
snippet: The Dragon does not die, if it is not killed by its brother and sister, that is to say by Sol and Luna. · Maier argues that the philosophical dragon — the unreformed prima materia — can not be killed by any single agent but only by the combined action of its own brother and sister, Sol and Luna (gold and silver, sulphur and mercury). He d

title: Emblem XXVI — Lady Sapientia / Tree of Life
snippet: The fruit of human wisdom is the Tree of Life. · Maier identifies the fruit of human wisdom as the Tree of Life it self, arguing that the philosopher's stone is the true 'wood of life' sought by sages across all traditions. He draws on Cicero's distinction between humans and animals — that only humans possess reason and the cap

title: Emblem XXVII — Companion to Sapientia sequence
snippet: He who tries to enter the Philosophical Rose-garden without the key is like a man who wants to walk without feet. · Maier argues that attempting to enter the Philosophical Rose-garden without the proper key is as futile as trying to walk without feet. He develops this as a warning against shortcuts in the alchemical work: the three 'cooki

title: Emblem XXVIII — King Duenech in steam bath
snippet: The king is bathed, sitting in a steam-bath, and is freed from the black bile by Pharut. · Maier develops the Allegoria Merlini's narrative of King Duenech, who is bathed in a steam-bath to purge the black bile that ails him. He argues that the king represents philosophical gold in its impure state, and the bath is the vessel in which dis

title: Emblem XXIX — Emblem XXIX
snippet: Just as the Salamander lives in the fire, so also the Stone. · Maier develops the ancient belief that the salamander lives in fire without being consumed, applying it as an allegory for the philosopher's stone, which endures the test of fire and emerges unchanged or improved. He argues that just as the salamander is nourished rather than

title: Emblem XXX — Emblem XXX
snippet: The Hermaphrodite, lying in darkness like a dead man, needs fire. · Maier argues that the Hermaphrodite — the unified philosophical substance combining masculine and feminine principles — lies inert in darkness until awakened by fire. He develops this as an allegory for the transition from nigredo (the death-like darkness) to the active s

title: Emblem XXXI — King transformation continued
snippet: The king, swimming in the sea, calling in a loud voice: He who saves me will receive a great reward. · Maier recounts the allegory of the king swimming in the sea who cries out that whoever saves him will receive a great reward. He develops this as a figure for philosophical gold dissolved in the mercurial sea — the noble substance submer

title: Emblem XXXII — Emblem XXXII
snippet: As coral grows under water and, exposed to the air, gets hard, so also the Stone. · Maier draws on the natural history of coral — soft and plant-like under water but hardening to stone when exposed to air — as an analogy for the philosopher's stone, which is fluid and mutable during preparation but becomes fixed and permanent once complet

title: Emblem XXXIII — Hermaphrodite lying in darkness needs fire
snippet: The Hermaphrodite, lying in darkness like a dead man, needs fire. · Maier returns to the Hermaphrodite-in-darkness motif, now explicitly connecting it to the practice of ritual purification through fire and water. He argues that divine origin was ascribed to certain individuals in antiquity through a process of trial — passing through fir

title: Emblem XXXIV — Celestial conception / hierogamy
snippet: He is conceived in the bath and born in the air, but having become red he walks upon the waters. · Maier develops the motto — 'He is conceived in the bath and born in the air' — as an allegory for the philosophical substance that begins in the liquid phase (conception in the bath) and emerges in the aerial or volatile state (birth in the

title: Emblem XXXV — Emblem XXXV
snippet: As Ceres accustomed Triptolemus, and Thetis Achilles, to endure fire, so the artist does with the Stone. · Maier draws on two parallel myths — Ceres hardening Triptolemus in fire and Thetis dipping Achilles in the Styx — to argue that the philosopher's stone, like a divine child, must be tempered through exposure to fire to achieve its fu

title: Emblem XXXVI — Emblem XXXVI
snippet: The Stone has been thrown on to the earth and lifted on to the mountains, and it dwells in the air and feeds in the river. · Maier develops the paradox that the philosopher's stone has been thrown on to the earth (despised, found in humble places) yet lifted on to mountains (exalted, valued above all things). He argues that the stone dwel

title: Emblem XXXVII — Emblem XXXVII
snippet: Three things are sufficient for mastery: white smoke, that is water, the green Lion, that is the Ore of Hermes, and stinking water. · Maier argues that mastery requires only three things: white smoke (water in its volatile form), the green lion (the raw ore of Hermes, vitriol or an impure metallic substance), and stinking water (the foul

title: Emblem XXXVIII — Standing hermaphrodite
snippet: The Hermaphrodite, born of two mountains, of Mercury and Venus. · Maier presents the standing Hermaphrodite — born of the two mountains of Mercury and Venus — as the completed product of the alchemical work. He develops Socrates' declaration that he was a citizen not of one city but of the world as an analogy for the Hermaphrodite who inh

title: Emblem XXXIX — Emblem XXXIX
snippet: Oedipus, having conquered the Sphinx and having killed his father Laius, married his mother Jocasta. · Maier retells the Oedipus myth as an alchemical allegory: conquering the Sphinx (solving the riddle of Nature) leads to killing the father (destroying the original metallic form) and marrying the mother (reuniting with the prima materia

title: Emblem XL — Emblem XL
snippet: Make one water out of two waters, and it will be the water of holiness. · Maier instructs the alchemist to make one water from two waters, producing the 'water of holiness.' He develops this as the final mercurial conjunction: the two philosophical waters — one volatile and ascending, the other fixed and descending — must be combined into

title: Emblem XLI — Emblem XLI
snippet: Adonis is killed by a boar, and Venus, rushing up to him, painted the roses red with her blood. · Maier retells the death of Adonis — beloved of Venus, killed by a boar, his blood dyeing white roses red — as an allegory for the transition from albedo to rubedo. He argues that the beautiful, white philosophical substance (Adonis/the white

title: Emblem XLII — Emblem XLII
snippet: What Nature, Reason, Experience and Reading teach you, let that be the fire, the vessel, the water and the earth. · Maier develops the Tabula Smaragdina's teaching by identifying four guides that together constitute the alchemical method: Nature provides the raw material and model, Reason supplies theoretical understanding, Experience off

title: Emblem XLIII — Emblem XLIII
snippet: Listen to the screech owl's voice, which it produces it self, and the water and do not heed any bird that cries in the evening. · Maier instructs the alchemist to listen to the screech owl's voice and the sound of running water while ignoring the cries of evening birds — a parable about discerning true signals from false. He develops the

title: Emblem XLIV — Osiris killed and resurrected
snippet: Typhon kills Osiris by a ruse, and after that he scatters his limbs far and wide, but Is is gathers them together again. · Maier retells the Egyptian myth of Osiris — murdered by Typhon through treachery, his body scattered, then painstakingly reassembled by Is is — as the complete arc of the alchemical opus. He identifies Typhon's killin

title: Emblem XLV — Emblem XLV
snippet: Sol and his shadow complete the work. · Maier argues that Sol and his shadow together complete the work — the visible, noble gold (Sol) must be joined with its shadowy, hidden counterpart (the impure, dark aspect) to produce the philosopher's stone. He develops the principle that the stone requires both the light and d

title: Emblem XLVI — Emblem XLVI
snippet: Two eagles meet, the one from the East, the other from the West. · Maier develops the meeting of two eagles — one from the East, the other from the West — as an allegory for the conjunction of the two philosophical principles that originate from opposite ends of the alchemical process. He identifies the Eastern eagle with sulphur (the hot

title: Emblem XLVII — Emblem XLVII
snippet: The wolf coming from the East, and the Dog coming from the West, have bitten each other. · Maier parallels the eagle meeting of Emblem XLVI with a more violent version: the wolf from the East and the dog from the West bite each other in mutual destruction. He argues that while the eagles' conjunction was aerial and spiritual, the wolf-and

title: Emblem XLVIII — King Duenech receives potion
snippet: The king, fallen ill from drinking, is restored to health by a physician. · Maier develops the Merlini allegory of King Duenech in its final phase: the king, fallen ill from drinking foul waters, is restored to health by physicians who administer the correct elixir. He draws on Xerxes drinking muddy water in the desert as an analogy for t

title: Emblem XLIX — Emblem XLIX
snippet: The Philosophical Child acknowledges three fathers, just as Orion. · Maier argues that the philosopher's stone, like the mythological Orion, acknowledges three fathers — three distinct sources or principles that contribute to its generation. He develops this as an allegory for the tria prima: the stone is born from salt (the bodily princi

title: Emblem L — The Dragon kills the woman
snippet: The Dragon kills the woman, and she kills it, and together they bathe in blood. · Maier narrates the final emblem as a double death: the dragon kills the woman and she kills it, and together they bathe in the blood of their mutual destruction. He develops this as the ultimate expression of the alchemical principle that the Stone is born f

title: Adonis
snippet: Dying god of vegetation beloved by Venus; represents the philosophical Sun that must die seasonally and be reborn through love's restorative power.

title: Albedo
snippet: The whitening; second stage involving washing, purification, removal of impurities.

title: Alchemical Dog (Canis)
snippet: The dog, one of the most familiar of all domestic animals, serves in alchemy as a figure for the mercurial principle in its western, cold, moist, dissolving aspect. In classical and medieval culture, the dog was associated with loyalty, guardianship, and the underworld (Cerberus, the three-headed ho

title: Alchemical Eagle (Aquila)
snippet: The eagle holds a commanding position in both classical mythology and alchemical symbolism. In Greek tradition, the eagle is the bird of Zeus, bearer of thunderbolts and transporter of Ganymede to Olympus. The eagle's capacity for high flight, its sharp vision, and its association with celestial pow

title: Alchemical Eucharist (Eucharistia Alchemica)
snippet: The identification of alchemical transmutation with the Catholic Communion rite; Szulakowska's key argument about Maier's religious dimension.

title: Alchemical Lion (Leo)
snippet: The lion is the most frequently invoked animal in the entire corpus of alchemical imagery, appearing in dozens of guises across centuries of Hermetic literature. In natural history and mythology, the lion signifies sovereignty, solar power, and indomitable strength. The lion's association with the s

title: Alchemical Rose / Rosa Philosophorum (Rosa Philosophorum)
snippet: The philosophical rose—white and red—central to the iconography of the Rosarium Philosophorum and the alchemical garden.

title: Alchemical Toad (Bufo)
snippet: The toad occupies a peculiar place in both natural history and folk belief. Cold, warty, earth-dwelling, and popularly believed to be poisonous, the toad was regarded in medieval and early modern culture as a creature of the lowest order, associated with darkness, venom, and the subterranean world.

title: Alchemical Wolf (Lupus)
snippet: The wolf occupies a singular place in medieval and early modern natural history as a creature of legendary voracity. Pliny and other classical authorities described the wolf as so ravenous that, failing to find prey, it would devour earth itself, growing so heavy it could hardly be knocked over. The

title: Alembic (Alembicum)
snippet: The alembic is the apparatus used for distillation, one of the most fundamental operations in both practical chemistry and alchemical philosophy. In its simplest form, the alembic consists of three parts: a cucurbit (the lower vessel containing the liquid to be distilled), a head or cap (the dome-sh

title: Allegoria Merlini
snippet: Medieval alchemical narrative in which a king suffering from a wasting disease is cured through purgative operations encoding the stages of the Work.

title: Apocalyptic Woman / Turkish Madonna (Mulier Apocalyptica)
snippet: The Woman of Revelation 12 — identified by Szulakowska as the Virgin Mary in Maier's alchemical Eucharist, connected to Ottoman war imagery.

title: Aqua Regia
snippet: Royal water; the only solvent capable of dissolving gold, composed of nitric and hydrochloric acids. Symbol of the power to dissolve the noblest metal.

title: Aqua Vitae
snippet: The water of life; the purified mercurial water that dissolves impure matter and vivifies the dead body of the Work.

title: Arbor Philosophica / Philosophical Tree (Arbor Philosophica)
snippet: The alchemical tree of metals or tree of life whose branches bear the seven planetary metals and whose fruit is the Stone.

title: Ars Magna
snippet: The combinatorial art attributed to Ramon Llull, adapted by pseudo-Lullian alchemists into a framework for understanding dissolution and coagulation as reciprocal operations.

title: Artis Auriferae (Artis Auriferae Quam Chemiam Vocant)
snippet: The Artis Auriferae, Quam Chemiam Vocant ("Of the Gold-Making Art, Which They Call Chemistry") is one of the most important alchemical anthologies ever assembled. First published in Basel in 1572 by Petrum Pernam in two volumes, it gathers dozens of foundational alchemical treatises into

title: Atalanta
snippet: The fleeing maiden of the frontispiece; represents volatile Mercury that must be captured by the fixed principle.

title: Athanor
snippet: The athanor is the alchemist's furnace, the controlled source of heat without which no transformation in the vessel can proceed. The word derives from the Arabic al-tannur (oven or bread furnace), and the device was designed to maintain a steady, even temperature over long periods, a critical requir

title: Aurora Consurgens
snippet: Late medieval alchemical text attributed to Pseudo-Aquinas, fusing Solomonic wisdom literature with alchemical symbolism through a Christological lens.

title: Aurum Philosophicum
snippet: Philosophical gold; not common gold but the perfected substance produced by the Work, embodying the solar principle in its highest form.

title: Aurum Potabile
snippet: Drinkable gold — the supreme alchemical medicine; Maier devoted his De Circulo Physico Quadrato (1616) to this concept.

title: Balneum / Alchemical Bath (Balneum)
snippet: The alchemical bath—balneum mariae or steam bath—in which the king is immersed for cure or the matter is gently heated.

title: Boreas
snippet: Greek god of the North Wind who carries the philosophical embryo in his belly; central figure of Emblem I.

title: Calcination (Calcinatio)
snippet: Heating to ash; burning away impurities. Part of the washing/purification complex.

title: Cantus Firmus
snippet: The cantus firmus, literally 'fixed song,' is a pre-existing melody that serves as the structural foundation upon which additional voices are composed in a polyphonic musical work. In medieval and Renaissance counterpoint, the cantus firmus provided a slow-moving, stable melodic line, typically in l

title: Catena Aurea
snippet: Golden Chain linking Microcosm to Macrocosm; continuous thread of transmutation from base to divine.

title: Cauda Pavonis
snippet: The Peacock's Tail; an iridescent display of colors appearing in the vessel during the transitional stage between nigredo and albedo, signaling that transformation is underway.

title: Celestial Dew / Ros Coelestis (Ros Coelestis)
snippet: The dew that falls from heaven, gathered as a privileged solvent and nourishing principle for the alchemical work.

title: Ceres
snippet: Earth goddess of grain and nourishment; represents the maternal, nourishing principle that sustains the philosophical child through the stages of the Work.

title: Circulatio
snippet: Circular distillation in which the volatile is repeatedly raised and returned, purifying the matter through cyclical repetition.

title: Citrinitas
snippet: The yellowing; intermediate stage of vivification between whiteness and redness.

title: Clangor Buccinae
snippet: The Clangor Buccinae ("Blast of the Trumpet") is a medieval Latin alchemical treatise of uncertain authorship, first printed in the collection Alchimia (De Alchimia Opuscula Complura Veterum Philosophorum, Frankfurt, 1550) and subsequently reprinted in Volume I of the <

title: Coagulation (Coagulatio)
snippet: Solidification; fixing the volatile. One nature coagulates only through dissolution of another.

title: Coniunctio (Coniunctio Oppositorum)
snippet: Union of opposites (male/female, volatile/fixed, Sun/Moon). The central operation of the Great Work.

title: Corpus Hermeticum
snippet: The Corpus Hermeticum is a collection of philosophical and theological treatises attributed to Hermes Trismegistus ("Thrice-Great Hermes"), the legendary Greco-Egyptian sage who was believed to be a contemporary of Moses and a conduit for the most ancient divine wisdom. The texts origina

title: Cybele
snippet: Phrygian mother goddess who transforms Atalanta and Hippomenes into lions as punishment for profaning her temple.

title: De Lapide Philosophico
snippet: Alchemical emblem book by Lambsprinck pairing symbolic engravings with verse explanations, a key prototype for the visual emblem tradition Maier inherits.

title: De Veritate Artis Chemicae (De Veritate et Antiquitate Artis Chemicae)
snippet: Sixteenth-century treatise by Robertus Vallensis defending the antiquity and legitimacy of alchemy, including an allegorical reading of the Atalanta myth.

title: Dissolution (Dissolutio)
snippet: Breaking down solid form into liquid; releasing the fixed into the volatile.

title: Distillatio
snippet: Separation of a substance by heating to vapor and condensing, purifying the volatile from the gross.

title: Divinae Institutiones
snippet: Patristic work by Lactantius on divine wisdom and the nourishment of the soul, providing Christian authority for the identification of alchemy with sacred knowledge.

title: Dragon (Draco)
snippet: Volatile spirit, guardian of treasure, and ouroboric figure of self-consuming transformation in alchemical iconography.

title: Dryad (Dryas)
snippet: Tree-nymph of Greek mythology; Rozenrichter identifies hidden dryads in AF emblems as feminine guiding forces for the alchemist.

title: Earth-Mother / Terra (Terra Mater)
snippet: The massive globular female earth-figure depicted reclining on the landscape in Emblems I and II as nurse of the philosophical infant.

title: Elixir
snippet: The perfected medicine capable of healing all disease and transmuting metals; synonymous with the Philosopher's Stone in its medicinal aspect.

title: Emblem Book (Liber Emblematum)
snippet: A genre of illustrated books combining motto, image, and epigram in a tripartite structure; AF is the most complex example with added music.

title: Fama Fraternitatis
snippet: First Rosicrucian manifesto (1614), announcing the Brotherhood of the Rose Cross and calling for a universal reformation of knowledge through alchemy and spiritual illumination.

title: Fermentatio
snippet: Fermentation; the penultimate operation that introduces the ferment completing the Stone's transformation.

title: Fixatio
snippet: Fixing the volatile; rendering the mercurial spirit permanent and stable through repeated operations.

title: Flaming Sword (Gladius Igneus)
snippet: The fiery sword that pierces the philosophical egg, introducing controlled heat into the sealed vessel of the work.

title: Four Elements (Quatuor Elementa)
snippet: The four elements, earth, water, air, and fire, constitute the fundamental classification of matter inherited from Aristotelian natural philosophy and adopted by the entire Western alchemical tradition. Empedocles first proposed the fourfold scheme in the fifth century BCE, and Aristotle refined it

title: Fugue (Fuga)
snippet: Three-voice musical canon accompanying each emblem. Voices represent Atalanta (fleeing/volatile), Hippomenes (pursuing/fixed), and Golden Apple (catalyst).

title: Garden of the Hesperides (Horti Hesperidum)
snippet: The Garden of the Hesperides is the mythological paradise at the western edge of the world where golden apples grow on a tree guarded by a never-sleeping dragon and tended by the Hesperides, the nymphs of the evening. In Greek mythology, Hercules must steal these golden apples as the eleventh of his

title: Geryon
snippet: Three-bodied king of Spain whose cattle Hercules stole as his tenth labor; in AF, a figure for the composite nature of the prima materia.

title: Golden Apple (Pomum Aureum)
snippet: The golden apple is the mythological object that gives the Atalanta Fugiens both its narrative framework and its deepest structural metaphor. In Ovid's Metamorphoses, the swift huntress Atalanta agrees to marry only the man who can outrun her; all who fail will die. Hippomenes defeats her not throug

title: Golden Fleece (Vellus Aureum)
snippet: The Golden Fleece is the prize that Jason and the Argonauts sought at the end of their perilous voyage to Colchis, and it is one of the classical myths most persistently interpreted as concealed alchemical allegory. In the Greek legend, the fleece of the golden ram hung in the grove of Ares, guarded

title: Green Lion (Leo Viridis)
snippet: The Green Lion is one of the most celebrated and debated symbols in the entire alchemical canon. Unlike the red lion (representing mature, fixed sulphur) or the paired winged and wingless lions of the mercury-sulphur theory, the Green Lion is a substance in its own right: the raw, unripened ore from

title: Hercules
snippet: Greek hero whose twelve labors include stealing the golden apples of the Hesperides; archetype of the alchemist in Maier's mytho-alchemy.

title: Hermaphrodite (Hermaphroditus)
snippet: The perfect union of male and female principles in one body. Represents the completed coniunctio.

title: Hermes Trismegistus
snippet: Hermes Trismegistus, the 'thrice-great Hermes,' is the legendary figure whom the Western esoteric tradition identifies as the primordial sage, the fountainhead from which all secret knowledge of nature flows. He is a syncretic creation, fusing the Greek messenger-god Hermes with the Egyptian god of

title: Hesperides
snippet: The three nymphs—Aegle, Arethusa, and Hespertusa—who guard the tree of golden apples in the far western garden.

title: Hierogamy / Sacred Marriage (Hieros Gamos)
snippet: The sacred marriage of opposites—Sol and Luna, king and queen, heaven and earth—central to alchemical and Hermetic transformation.

title: Hippomenes
snippet: The pursuer in the frontispiece race; represents fixed Sulphur pursuing volatile Mercury with golden apples as catalysts.

title: Isis
snippet: Egyptian goddess who reassembles the dismembered Osiris; represents the lunar anima that restores the dissolved solar principle to wholeness.

title: Jason (Iason)
snippet: Greek hero who quests for the Golden Fleece, slaying the dragon that guards it; alchemical archetype of the seeker.

title: King Duenech (Rex Duenech)
snippet: Alchemical king figure suffering from melancholic disease. Cured through purification. Appears in XXVIII and XLVIII.

title: Kronos / Saturn (Saturnus)
snippet: Titan who devours his children and vomits the stone; alchemical figure for dissolution, nigredo, and the prima materia.

title: Lac Virginis
snippet: Virgin's milk — a volatile alchemical spirit considered a form of the quintessence, used in the first stage of creating the philosopher's stone.

title: Lapis
snippet: Shorthand for Lapis Philosophorum; the perfected Stone that transmutes base metals and heals all disease.

title: Latona
snippet: Goddess identified with the impure base metal (laton/latten) that must be whitened; represents matter in its unworked, impure state.

title: Liber Sapientiae
snippet: The biblical Book of Wisdom (Wisdom of Solomon), part of the Solomonic tradition linking divine wisdom to alchemical knowledge.

title: Luna
snippet: Silver; the lunar principle; the feminine receptive agent in alchemical transmutation.

title: Lusus Serius
snippet: The 'serious game' — Maier's term for the playful-yet-profound engagement with alchemical wisdom through emblems, music, and riddles.

title: Margarita Pretiosa Novella
snippet: The Margarita Pretiosa Novella ("The New Pearl of Great Price") is one of the most remarkable alchemical treatises of the fourteenth century. It was composed in 1330 by Petrus Bonus Lombardus of Ferrara, a physician, and first published in print by Janus Lacinius through the press of Ald

title: Medea
snippet: Sorceress of Greek mythology who rejuvenates old Aeson by boiling him in a cauldron; model for alchemical rejuvenation in Emblem IX.

title: Mercurius (Mercurius Philosophorum)
snippet: Mercury; the volatile, feminine, lunar principle. Liquid primary matter, primordial water, water of destruction and resurrection.

title: Mercury-Sulphur Theory (Theoria Mercurii et Sulphuris)
snippet: The mercury-sulphur theory is the foundational doctrine of medieval alchemy, asserting that all metals are composed of two principles: mercury and sulphur. In this framework, mercury represents the cold, moist, volatile, and feminine principle, while sulphur represents the hot, dry, fixed, and mascu

title: Metamorphoses
snippet: Ovid's Latin epic poem of mythological transformations, providing the Atalanta-Hippomenes narrative that Maier adopts as the overarching frame story for AF.

title: Monas Hieroglyphica
snippet: The Monas Hieroglyphica ("The Hieroglyphic Monad") is a cryptic mathematical-alchemical treatise written by the English polymath John Dee (1527-1607) and first published at Antwerp in 1564. Dee was an astrologer, alchemist, magician, and mathematician who enjoyed the protection of Queen

title: Mortificatio
snippet: The killing or death of the old form; the destruction of existing structure as prerequisite for renewal. Closely related to putrefaction and nigredo.

title: Multiplicatio
snippet: Multiplying the Stone's power; the final augmentation that increases the perfected product's transmutative capacity.

title: Musica Speculativa
snippet: The philosophical contemplation of musical harmony as a mirror of cosmic order; the tradition within which Maier's fugues operate.

title: Mytho-Alchemy (Mytho-Alchemia)
snippet: The practice of reading classical mythology as encoded alchemical teaching; Maier is identified by Forshaw as its 'main figurehead.'

title: Naaman (Naaman Syrus)
snippet: The Syrian military commander healed of leprosy by washing seven times in the Jordan River; alchemical figure for purification through repeated washing.

title: Nigredo
snippet: The blackening; first stage of the Work involving putrefaction, decomposition, death of the old form.

title: Oedipus
snippet: Solver of the Sphinx's riddle; represents the alchemical adept who must decode nature's obscure language to proceed in the Work.

title: Opus Magnum
snippet: The Great Work; the entire alchemical project of transmutation from base matter to the perfected Stone.

title: Osiris
snippet: Egyptian god of death and resurrection; in alchemical allegory, represents the solar principle that must be dismembered and reconstituted.

title: Ouroboros
snippet: The ouroboros, the serpent or dragon that devours its own tail, is among the oldest and most universal symbols in human culture. It appears in Egyptian funerary texts, in Gnostic cosmology, in Norse mythology as Jormungandr, and in Hindu and Buddhist iconography. The image conveys cyclical time, sel

title: Pallas Athena
snippet: The Greek goddess of wisdom, born fully armed from the head of Zeus; in the AF, she emerges with the descent of golden rain.

title: Paracelsus (Theophrastus von Hohenheim)
snippet: Theophrastus von Hohenheim (1493-1541), known universally as Paracelsus, was a Swiss-German physician, alchemist, and natural philosopher whose radical reforms transformed both medicine and alchemy in the sixteenth century. Born the son of a physician, Paracelsus trained at the mines of Sigismund Fu

title: Pelicanus
snippet: The Pelican vessel, a circulatory apparatus in which distillate flows back onto the matter below; also the symbol of self-sacrifice and self-nourishment.

title: Philosopher's Stone (Lapis Philosophorum)
snippet: The perfected product of the Great Work; agent of transmutation. Achieved through union and fixation.

title: Philosophical Bird (Avis Philosophica)
snippet: The bird is perhaps the most versatile category of creature in alchemical symbolism, encompassing the raven of the nigredo, the peacock of the iridescent middle stage, the swan or dove of the albedo, and the phoenix of the rubedo. But beyond these specific avian symbols, alchemical writers speak of

title: Philosophical Child / Puer Philosophicus (Puer Philosophicus)
snippet: The infant figure—newborn, swaddled, or growing—who appears throughout the AF as the nascent Stone in personified form.

title: Philosophical Egg (Ovum Philosophicum)
snippet: The sealed vessel containing prima materia; unity of opposites before differentiation.

title: Prima Materia
snippet: The first matter from which all metals arise; the undifferentiated starting material of the Work.

title: Prisca Sapientia
snippet: The 'ancient wisdom' or perennial philosophy transmitted from Hermes Trismegistus through successive mystery schools to the modern age.

title: Projectio
snippet: The final application of the perfected Stone onto base metal to effect transmutation; the culminating act of the Great Work.

title: Putrefaction (Putrefactio)
snippet: Controlled decomposition; the death of old form that enables new creation. Associated with nigredo.

title: Quadrivium
snippet: The four mathematical disciplines of medieval university education: arithmetic, music, geometry, and astronomy.

title: Quintessence (Quinta Essentia)
snippet: The quintessence, or quinta essentia, is the fifth element of the philosophical tradition, the substance that transcends and perfects the four terrestrial elements of earth, water, air, and fire. Aristotle introduced the concept as the aether, the incorruptible material of the celestial spheres, dis

title: Raven / Crow (Corvus)
snippet: The raven and the crow, often treated as interchangeable in alchemical texts, are the pre-eminent birds of the nigredo, the black initial stage of the alchemical work. In natural history, both birds are noted for their intelligence, their black plumage, and their association with death and carrion.

title: Rebis
snippet: The 'two-thing' (res bina); the reunited opposites in a single substance, combining masculine and feminine, fixed and volatile in stable union.

title: Regina
snippet: The Queen figure representing Luna, silver, or the feminine volatile principle in alchemical allegory.

title: Rex
snippet: The King figure representing Sol, gold, or the masculine fixed principle in alchemical allegory.

title: Rosarium Philosophorum
snippet: Rose Garden of Philosophers; major alchemical compilation. Source for geometric, sapiential, and washing themes.

title: Rosicrucian Brotherhood (Fraternitas Rosae Crucis)
snippet: The Rosicrucian Brotherhood, or Fraternitas Rosae Crucis, is the secret society whose manifestos burst upon the German-speaking world in 1614-1616 and whose ideals profoundly shaped both Michael Maier's career and the intellectual context of the Atalanta Fugiens. The movement announced itself throug

title: Rubedo
snippet: The reddening; final stage of completion, perfection, achievement of the Philosopher's Stone.

title: Salamander (Salamandra)
snippet: The salamander is one of the most enduring creatures of pre-modern natural history, believed since antiquity to be capable of living in fire without being consumed. Pliny, Aristotle, and medieval bestiaries all described this small, lizard-like amphibian as possessing a cold, moist constitution so e

title: Sapientia
snippet: Wisdom personified; alchemy as knowledge joined with practice. Central to Emblem XXVI.

title: Scala Philosophorum
snippet: The Scala Philosophorum ("Ladder of the Philosophers") is a medieval Latin alchemical treatise printed in Volume II of the Artis Auriferae (Basel, 1572), beginning at page 115. Its author is probably Guido de Montanor, who lived in the fourteenth or fifteenth century, judging by

title: Screech Owl (Noctua)
snippet: The owl has been a creature of ambivalent symbolism since antiquity. In Greek tradition, the owl was sacred to Athena, goddess of wisdom, and the little owl (Athene noctua) appeared on Athenian coins as a symbol of knowledge and discernment. Yet the owl was also a creature of the night, associated w

title: Secretum Nobilissimorum
snippet: Sixteenth-century alchemical text by Jodocus Greverus containing allegorical readings of classical myths as encoded alchemical teachings.

title: Sol
snippet: Gold; the solar principle; the masculine active agent in alchemical transmutation.

title: Solutio
snippet: Dissolving of a solid into liquid; the return of differentiated matter to a fluid, undifferentiated state.

title: Solve et Coagula
snippet: Dissolve and coagulate; the fundamental binary rhythm of the alchemical Work, alternating between breaking down and building up.

title: Sphinx
snippet: Mythological guardian who poses riddles and destroys those who cannot answer; represents the obscurity of alchemical doctrine that tests the adept.

title: Splendor Solis
snippet: The Splendor Solis ("Splendour of the Sun") is one of the most visually magnificent alchemical manuscripts of the Renaissance. Attributed to Salomon Trismosin, a legendary figure said to have been the teacher of Paracelsus, the work is probably pseudonymous, with its earliest manuscripts

title: Steganography (Steganographia)
snippet: The art of concealing messages within an apparently innocent cover text or image; Bilak argues AF contains a hidden magic square.

title: Sublimatio
snippet: Raising the gross to the subtle; purification through elevation of volatile spirits from dense matter.

title: Sulphur
snippet: The fixed, masculine, solar principle. Paired with Mercury as the two fundamental alchemical substances.

title: Summa Perfectionis (Summa Perfectionis Magisterii)
snippet: The Summa Perfectionis Magisterii ("The Sum of the Perfection of the Mastery") is one of the most important alchemical treatises of the Middle Ages. It is attributed to Geber (Geber Latinus), a Latin author long identified with the Arabic alchemist Jabir ibn Hayyan, though modern scholar

title: Tabula Chimica
snippet: Latin translation of an alchemical work by Muhammad ibn Umail (Senior Zadith), containing visionary imagery of volatile and fixed principles as paired birds.

title: Tabula Smaragdina
snippet: The Emerald Tablet; foundational Hermetic text attributed to Hermes Trismegistus. 'As above, so below' cosmology.

title: Tinctura
snippet: The Tincture; the coloring agent of transmutation that imparts the golden or red color to base metal, synonymous with the perfected Stone in its active mode.

title: Tractatulus de Practica Lapidis (Tractatulus de Practica Lapidis Philosophici)
snippet: Short Latin alchemical treatise falsely attributed to Aristotle, transmitted through the Artis Auriferae compilation. Source for elemental union imagery.

title: Tria Prima
snippet: Paracelsian three principles: Mercury, Sulphur, Salt. Tilton argues AF should not be reduced to this framework.

title: Triptolemus / Achilles in the Fire (Triptolemus)
snippet: The infant held over flames by Ceres (or Thetis) to make him immortal—a mythic figure of the philosophical child tempered in fire.

title: Turba Philosophorum
snippet: Assembly of Philosophers; major alchemical compilation of Arabic origin. Source for 9 AF mottos per De Jong.

title: Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine (Duodecim Claves Basilii Valentini)
snippet: The Twelve Keys (Practica cum Duodecim Clavibus et Appendice, De Magno Lapide Antiquorum Sapientium) is an alchemical treatise attributed to Basil Valentine (Basilius Valentinus), a figure said to have been a Benedictine monk. The work was first published by Michael Maier himsel

title: Typhon
snippet: Egyptian destroyer who dismembers Osiris; represents the corrosive, fiery agent of dissolution that breaks down the old form.

title: Ulysses / Odysseus (Ulysses)
snippet: Greek hero of endurance and wisdom; Maier's self-identification in his final work, representing the alchemist who perseveres through shipwreck.

title: Vas Hermeticum
snippet: The hermetically sealed vessel; the closed system in which all alchemical transformation occurs.

title: Venus
snippet: Goddess of love who provides the golden apples enabling Hippomenes to capture Atalanta; represents the catalytic feminine principle that makes union possible.

title: Visio Arislei
snippet: The Visio Arislei ("Vision of Arisleus") is a Latin alchemical allegory of Arabic origin, printed in Volume I of the Artis Auriferae (Basel, 1572) at pages 159-162. It belongs to the Turba Philosophorum literature and appears in the earliest known Latin manuscripts of t

title: Vitriol (Vitriolum)
snippet: Vitriol is one of the most practically important substances in the alchemical laboratory and one of the most symbolically charged terms in the alchemical vocabulary. In chemical terms, vitriol refers to a class of metallic sulfates: green vitriol is ferrous sulfate (iron sulfate), blue vitriol is co

title: White Lead (Plumbum Album)
snippet: Purified matter; the white earth into which gold must be sown. Product of the albedo stage.

title: Wild Boar / Aper (Aper)
snippet: The wild boar that gores Adonis to death in classical myth, recast by Maier as a destructive principle whose violence catalyzes transformation.

title: Adam McLean
snippet: Alchemical texts and symbolism, editing, publishing · Published Godwin AF edition; Alchemy Website with AF texts and hand-coloured plates · Adam McLean (born 7 March 1948, Glasgow) is a Scottish writer, editor, and publisher who has done more than any other individual to make alchemical texts and imagery accessible to a modern English-spe

title: Amber Rozenrichter

title: Catherine Morris Wescott
snippet: Music and Alchemy · Modal analysis of Maier's fugues as text-painting for alchemical Putrefaction and Albification · Catherine Morris Wescott is a scholar of music and alchemy whose analysis of the Atalanta Fugiens fugues, published in the AthanorX journal, provides the most technically detailed examination of Maier's musical compositions

title: Donna Bilak
snippet: Art history, steganography · Co-editor of Furnace and Fugue; Maier and steganography · Donna Bilak is an independent scholar of early modern visual culture, currently affiliated with NYU's Gallatin School of Individualized Study and formerly at the Pratt Institute. Her research centers on steganography, visual cryptography, a

title: Douglas Leedy
snippet: Musicology · Musical construction of the 50 canons · Douglas Leedy (1938-2015) was an American composer, conductor, and musicologist who specialized in early music performance practice, alternative tuning systems, and the intersection of music with cosmological and philosophical traditions. H

title: Hereward Tilton
snippet: History of alchemy, Rosicrucianism, historiography · Spiritual alchemy vs. laboratory debate; philosophical positioning of Maier · Hereward Tilton is a lecturer in the Department of Studies in Religion at the University of Queensland, Brisbane, Australia. His monograph The Quest for the Phoenix: Spiritual Alchemy and Rosicrucianism in the

Craven, D.D., was a Scottish clergyman and antiquarian from Kirkwall, Orkney, whose Count Michael Maier, Doctor of Philosophy and of Medicine, Alchemist, Rosicrucian, Mystic, 1568-1622, published by William Peace and Son i

snippet: Musicology, performance studies · AF as early multimedia and performative work · Johann F.W. Hasler is a musicologist whose article 'Performative and Multimedia Aspects of Late-Renaissance Meditative Alchemy: The Case of Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens (1617)' (Universitas Humanistica, 2011) provides the most sustained

title: Joscelyn Godwin
snippet: Musicology, esoteric studies · Musical edition and transcription of all 50 fugues · Joscelyn Godwin (born 16 January 1945, Kelmscott, Oxfordshire) is a musicologist, historian of esotericism, and translator. Educated as a chorister at Christ Church Cathedral School, Oxford, then at Radley College (Music Scholar), and Magda

title: Kathleen Perry Long
snippet: Early modern literature, music and medicine · Music as meditative and therapeutic practice in AF · Kathleen Perry Long is a scholar of early modern literature and intellectual history whose article 'Music and Meditative Practices in Early Modern Alchemy: The Example of the Atalanta fugiens' (Lo Sguardo, 2012) brings neuroscientific persp

title: Pamela H. Smith
snippet: History of science, material culture · AF as symbolic synthesis, not laboratory manual · Pamela H. Smith is the Seth Low Professor of History at Columbia University, founding Director of the Center for Science and Society, and principal investigator of the Making and Knowing Project. She is one of the most influential historian

title: Paul Miner
snippet: Literary history, iconography · AF influence on William Blake; hermaphrodite emblems · Paul Miner is a literary historian and iconographer whose article "Blake and Atalanta Fugiens: Two Plates, Three Conjectures," published in Notes and Queries (2012), investigates the visual influence of Maier's Merian-engraved plates on the

title: Peter J. Forshaw
snippet: Western esotericism, alchemy, Kabbalah · Mytho-alchemy, quadrivium structure, alchemical music · Peter J. Forshaw is Universitair Docent (Senior Lecturer) for History of Western Esotericism in the Early Modern Period at the Center for History of Hermetic Philosophy and Related Currents (GHF), University of Amsterdam. He studied Sanskri

title: Sarah Lang
snippet: Digital humanities, history of alchemy · RIDE review of Furnace and Fugue; computational approaches to AF · Sarah Lang is a digital humanities scholar at the University of Graz whose work bridges computational methods and the history of alchemy. Her RIDE review of the Furnace and Fugue digital edition (2022) provides the most detailed sch

title: Tara Nummedal
snippet: History of science, digital humanities · Co-editor of Furnace and Fugue digital edition · Tara Nummedal is the John Nickoll Provost's Professor of History and Professor of Italian Studies at Brown University. She is a historian of early modern alchemy, fraud, and entrepreneurship whose work has reshaped understanding of alchemy'

title: Urszula Szulakowska
snippet: Art history, alchemical illustration, religious politics · Religious-political context of AF; Eucharistic symbolism; Turkish Madonna · Urszula Szulakowska is an art historian whose three monographs — The Sacrificial Body and the Day of Doom (Brill), The Alchemy of Light (Brill), and The Alchemical Virgin Mary (Cambridge Scholars) — place

title: Walter Pagel
snippet: History of medicine, medical alchemy · Medical/humoral dimensions of AF emblems · Walter Pagel (1898-1983) was a German-born British pathologist and historian of medicine, the youngest son of Julius Pagel, Professor of Medical History at the Friedrich Wilhelms Universitat in Berlin. Pagel trained in pathology at the Muni

title: Aurora Consurgens
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · The Aurora Consurgens is a late medieval alchemical text attributed (probably falsely) to Thomas Aquinas, remarkable for its systematic fusion of Solomonic wisdom literature with alchemical symbolism through a Christological lens. De Jong (1969) shows that Mai

title: Jodocus Greverus, Secretum Nobilissimorum
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · Jodocus Greverus's Secretum Nobilissimorum is a sixteenth-century alchemical text that includes allegorical readings of classical myths as encoded alchemical teachings, contributing to the tradition of mythological alchemy that Maier inherits. De Jong (1969) i

title: Lactantius
snippet: PATRISTIC · Lactantius is a fourth-century Christian apologist whose writings on divine wisdom and the nourishment of the soul provided patristic authority for the identification of alchemical knowledge with spiritual sustenance. De Jong (1969) identifies Lactantius as on

title: Lambsprinck, De Lapide Philosophico
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · Lambsprinck's De Lapide Philosophico is a late sixteenth-century alchemical emblem book that pairs symbolic engravings with verse explanations, establishing a visual tradition of alchemical emblems upon which Maier builds. De Jong (1969) and Pagel (1973) both

title: Lullius tradition (via Harmonica Chemica)
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · The Pseudo-Lullian alchemical tradition, transmitted through compilations such as the Harmonica Chemica, attributes a body of practical and theoretical alchemical writings to the Catalan philosopher Ramon Llull, though most are late medieval pseudepigrapha. De

title: Merlini Allegory
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · The Merlini Allegory is a medieval alchemical narrative in which a king suffering from a wasting disease is cured through a sequence of purgative and transformative operations, serving as an extended parable for the purification of base metal. De Jong first an

title: Ovid, Metamorphoses X.560-704
snippet: CLASSICAL · Ovid's Metamorphoses, composed in the first century CE, provides the mythological narrative of Atalanta and Hippomenes that Maier adopts as the overarching frame story for his entire emblem book. De Jong (1969) demonstrates that Maier reads the footrace allego

title: Pseudo-Aristotle, Tractatulus de Practica Lapidis
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · The Tractatulus de Practica Lapidis Philosophici is a short Latin alchemical treatise falsely attributed to Aristotle, transmitted through the Artis Auriferae compilation and widely known in early modern alchemical circles. De Jong (1969) identifies it as the

title: Robertus Vallensis, De Veritate et Antiquitate Artis Chemicae
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · Robertus Vallensis's De Veritate et Antiquitate Artis Chemicae is a sixteenth-century treatise defending the antiquity and legitimacy of alchemy, which includes an allegorical interpretation of the Atalanta myth as an alchemical parable. De Jong (1969) identif

title: Rosarium Philosophorum
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · The Rosarium Philosophorum (Rose Garden of Philosophers) is a major fourteenth-century alchemical compilation attributed to Pseudo-Arnald of Villanova, widely circulated in manuscript and print as a compendium of practical and symbolic alchemical wisdom. De Jo

title: Rosicrucian Manifestarian Tradition
snippet: MOVEMENT · The Rosicrucian manifestarian tradition, inaugurated by the publication of the Fama Fraternitatis (1614) and Confessio Fraternitatis (1615), proposed a universal reformation of knowledge through the synthesis of alchemy, medicine, and spiritual illumination. T

title: Senior Zadith (Muhammad ibn Umail), Tabula Chimica
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · The Tabula Chimica is a Latin translation of an alchemical work by the tenth-century Arabic author Muhammad ibn Umail, known in the Latin West as Senior Zadith, whose visionary imagery profoundly influenced medieval and early modern alchemical symbolism. De Jo

title: Solomonic / Biblical Wisdom Tradition (Proverbs)
snippet: BIBLICAL · The Solomonic wisdom tradition, centered on the Book of Proverbs and its personification of Wisdom as a tree of life, provides the religious-philosophical foundation for the sapiential dimension of Atalanta Fugiens. De Jong (1969) demonstrates that the motto o

title: Tabula Smaragdina (Emerald Tablet)
snippet: HERMETIC · The Tabula Smaragdina, or Emerald Tablet, is a brief Hermetic text attributed to Hermes Trismegistus that circulated widely in Latin translation from the twelfth century onward, encoding the foundational principle that operations in the macrocosm mirror those

title: Turba Philosophorum
snippet: ALCHEMICAL · The Turba Philosophorum (Assembly of Philosophers) is a Latin translation of an Arabic alchemical compilation dating to approximately the ninth century, presenting alchemical doctrine as a series of speeches by ancient sages. De Jong (1969) identifies it as th

snippet: How One Scholar Unlocked Fifty Alchemical Emblems

snippet: Psychological Alchemy and Its Critics

snippet: An Aristotelian Among the Paracelsians
